title: HTML/XML Tag Objects
subtitle: it / at — operate inside an HTML or XML tag pair.
summary: it is the inner range between matching <tag>…</tag> pairs. at includes the tags themselves.

blocks.text: If you spend any time in markup, {key:i}{key:t} and {key:a}{key:t} pay rent. They jump out from the cursor to the nearest enclosing tag pair.

blocks.label: Change the contents of the surrounding tag
Delete the entire tag pair (open + content + close)

blocks.title: How Tag Matching Works
blocks.text:
Vim walks outward from the cursor looking for `` and the matching ``. Matching is *name-based* — Vim reads the tag name and finds the corresponding closer at the same nesting level. It understands self-closing tags (`
`) and attribute-rich openers, but it's a regex match — not an HTML parser. It can be fooled by: • Comments containing tag-like text. • Quoted strings containing `<` or `>`. • Truly broken markup. For 95% of real-world code it does the right thing. For the other 5%, fix the markup. {key:i}{key:t} excludes the opening and closing tags; {key:a}{key:t} includes both.
JSX, Vue, and other HTML-in-code templates: {key:i}{key:t} and {key:a}{key:t} usually still work — Vim doesn't know JSX, but `<Component>` and `</Component>` look the same to the regex matcher.
